results.possibleIdentity: David Lyle was a systems researcher, technical writer, and engineer based in Wanaque, New Jersey, who was active during the late 1950s through the early 1970s. He was the director of Increment Research Incorporated and was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that facilitated collaborations between artists and the technical community. Lyle's work was characterized by a highly idiosyncratic approach to technical fields, specifically focusing on large-scale language systems and the application of systems theory to social and ecological issues. He gained significant public attention for a series of articles in Esquire magazine in the late 1960s, including "The Logistics of Junk" (1966) and "The Human Race Has, Maybe, Thirty-five Years Left" (1967), the latter of which used computer-based metaphors such as "feedback" and "energy flow" to analyze global population crises. Earlier in his career, he was employed by Wright Aeronautical in New Jersey and was a member of the United Auto Workers (Local 669), where he authored letters exploring the concept of language as a "device" for the control of "human energy."
